content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_haec_p141_l13">,
							etc., i.e. if the consulship has been given me on these terms. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_perferrem_p141_l14">,
							subst. clause of purpose in apposition with <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							condicio</foreign>. <pb n="404"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_consul_p142_l2">
							, <gloss>I am a consul</gloss> (i.e. that kind of consul). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aequitas_p142_l3">:
							in the Forum was the tribunal of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign>
							who administered justice between citizens. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="campus_p142_l4">:
							see note on Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.11">11</ref> (<ref n="pl104.7">p. 104, l. 7</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auspicus_p142_l4">:
							the taking of the auspices always preceded the election. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auxilium_p142_l5">:
							the Roman Senate, having the management of foreign affairs, was at this
							time a great court of appeal for subject or friendly nations. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sedes_lionoris_p142_l7">,
							i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">sella curulis</foreign> or seat used by
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curule</foreign> magistrates: viz. interrex,
							dictator, <foreign xml:lang="lat">magister equitum</foreign>, consul, <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> censor, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								curule</foreign> aedile. It was like a modern camp-stool without
							back or sides, with crossed legs of ivory, so that it could be folded up
							and carried with the magistrate wherever he went. The bracketed words <foreign xml:lang="lat">sella curalis</foreign> are doubtless an explanatory
							marginal note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_p142_l8">: we
							should expect the subj. of characteristic, but the indic. is used (as
							often) to emphasize the <gloss>fact</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_eriperem_p142_l11">
							(l.15, below): subst. clause of result, in appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">exitum</foreign> (l.10). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foedissima_p142_l14">
							, <gloss>horrible</gloss>, with the added idea of polluting things
							sacred. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sabeatur_p142_l16">,
							hortatory subjunctive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fatale_p142_l17">:
							see Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.9">9</ref> (p.130). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="laeter_p142_l18">:
							§ <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 466; H. 559, 4 (484, v);
							H-B. 513, I: <foreign xml:lang="lat">apodosis</foreign>, see § <ref n="AG 515">515</ref>, a (306, a); G. 595; H. 586 (508, 4); H-B.
							582, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_mereor_p142_l24">
							, <gloss>in proportion as 1 deserve</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relaturos_gratiam_p142_l25">
							, <gloss>will reward</gloss> ("return favor": cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">habere, agere</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immatura_p142_l27">:
							because an ex-consul had reached the highest point of Roman ambition. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="misera_p142_l28">:
							the philosophy of the ancients professed to make them despise death (see
							Plato, Apologia, and Cicero, Tusc. Quaest. 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_qui_p142_l28">
							, <gloss>so iron-hearted as</gloss> (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								movear</foreign>, subj.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fratris_p142_l28">:
							his brother Quintus, younger than he, and at this time <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> elect. He served with credit in
							Caesar's Gallic campaigns. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neqae_non_p142_l31">
							, <gloss>nor can it be but that</gloss>, etc.: the two negatives make an
							affirmative, but with a kind of emphasis which the simple affirmative
							statement could not give. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="axor_p142_l32">:
							his wife Terentia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="filia_p142_l32">:
							his daughter Tullia, married to C. Calpurnius Piso. Daughters took the
							gentile name of the father; see § <ref n="AG 108">108</ref>, b (So,
							c); G. P- 493; H. 354, 9 (649, 4); H.-B. 678,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="filias_p142_l32">:
							his son Marcus, now two years old. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gener_p143_l3">:
							Piso was not yet a member of the Senate, and was probably standing in
							the lobby. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="moveor_p143_l3">
							(emphat., as shown by its position), <gloss>I am affected</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ati_sint_p143_l4">,
							[to wish] <gloss>that</gloss>, etc. (the verb being implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">moveor</foreign>); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pereamus_p143_l4">
							is in the same construction as <foreign xml:lang="lat">sint</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ana_peste_p143_l5">,
							i.e. by a destruction which is at the same time that of the whole state. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_Gracchus_p143_l9">,
							etc.: to preserve the emphasis, render <gloss>it</gloss>
							<gloss>is
								not
								Ti. Gracchus who</gloss>, etc. For the historical allusions, cf.
							Cat. 1, sects. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 4.3">3</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Catil. 4.4">4</ref> (p. 100), and notes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agrarios_p143_l10">:
							see note on <ref n="pl147.29">p. 147, l. 29</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Memmium_p143_l11">:
							C. Memmius, one of the most upright men of his time; he was a candidate
							for the consulship against Glaucia, and was murdered by instigation of
							Glaucia and Saturninus (B.C. ion). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tenentur_p143_l13">
							, <gloss>are in custody</gloss>: to preserve the emphasis we may Change
							the voice, - <gloss>we have in our hands</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vestram_Omnium_p143_l13">:
							§ <ref n="AG 302">302</ref>, e (184, d); B. 243,3, a; G.321, R.2; H-B.
							339, b. <pb n="406"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_nemo_p143_l17">:
							instead of the usual <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne quis</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne quisquam</foreign> because of the following <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">quidem</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 538">538</ref>, 310,
							a, b (319, d, R., cf. 105, d, N.); G. 543, 4, cf. 317, I; H. 568, Cf.
							513 (497, ii, cf. 457); Cf. H.-B. 276, I and 7. <milestone n="3" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicils_p143_l22">:
							their verdict on the conspirators' guilt consisted in the acts recounted
							in the following clauses. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gratias_egistis_p143_l23">:
							Cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">relaturos</foreign>, <ref n="pl142.25">p.
								142, l. 25</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abdicare_p143_l25">,
							etc.: see Cat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign> sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 4.14">14</ref> (<ref n="pl133.4">p. 133, l.
								4</ref>), and note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sed_p144_l1">,
							i.e. though you have in fact decided. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamquam_integrum_p144_l1">
							, <gloss>as if an open question</gloss> (i.e. as if you had not already
							expressed your judgment). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicetis_p144_l2">
							refers to their <gloss>judgment</gloss> as a court with respect to the
							facts; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="censeatis_p144_l2">,
							to their <gloss>view</gloss> as a public council respecting the
							punishment. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_consulis_p144_l3">,
							etc., <gloss>I will say in advance what belongs to</gloss> [me as] <gloss>the
							consul</gloss>: i.e. declare the need of instant action; <gloss>
								what</gloss> action, it is for the Senate to determine; for
							construction, see § <ref n="AG 343">343</ref>,4 (214, c); G. 366; H.
							439 (401); H.-B. 340. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nova_misceri_p144_l4">
							, <gloss>that a revolution subversive of the government was on
								foot</gloss> ; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nova_p144_l4">
							(subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="misceri_p144_l4">)
							means <gloss>innovations</gloss> or <gloss>unconstitutional
								measures</gloss> ; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="misceri_p144_l4_2">
							refers to the disorder which these would produce. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="concitari_mala_p144_l5">
							, <gloss>that evil designs were set in motion</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videbam_p144_l5">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, b (277, b); B. 260, 4; G.
							234; H. 535(469, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opinione_p144_l11">:
							§ <ref n="AG 406">406</ref>,a(247, b); B.217,4; G.398,N.1; H. 471,7
							(417, 1, N.1); H.-B. 416, e. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="provincias_p144_l13">,
							especially Spain, with which Cn. Piso had had relations. It had not yet
							become fully reconciled since the overthrow of Sertorius, only eight
							years before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sustentando_p144_l13">
							, <gloss>by forbearance</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prolatando_p144_l13">
							, <gloss>by procrastination</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratione_p144_l14">:
							abl. of manner. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placet_p144_l15">,
							sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">vindicare</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_p144_l17">
							(with a gesture), <gloss>all this</gloss>, i.e. city, citizens, and
							government. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplectitur_p144_l19">
							, <gloss>adopts</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_p144_l20"> , <gloss>in
							accordance with</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versatur_in_p144_l21">
							, <gloss>exhibits</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mortem_p144_l27">,
							etc.: a doctrine of the Epicureans, to which sect Caesar and many other
							eminent Romans belonged. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="et_ea_p144_l31">:
							cf. note on <ref n="pl136.17">p. 136, l. 17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municipus_dispertiri_p144_l32">,
							Sc. <gloss>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">eos</foreign> in <foreign xml:lang="lat">
									custodiam</foreign>
							</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iniquitatem_p145_l1">,
							since it might expose them to danger, and it would be unjust to choose
							among so many; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="difficultatem_p145_l1">,
							since they might decline the service. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="statueritis_p145_l3">:
							subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dignitatis_p145_l4">:
							§ <ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, c (214, d) ; cf. B. 198, 3; G. 366, R.1;
							H 439, 3 (401, N.2); cf. H.-B. 340. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adjungit_p145_l4">
							, <gloss>he</gloss> [Caesar] <gloss>adds</gloss> to his proposal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ruperit_p145_l5">:
							§ <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 2 (341, c) ; cf. B. 323; G. 366; H. 439
							(401); H.-B. 536, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sancit_p145_l7"> , <gloss>
							ordains</gloss> (under penalties). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_senatum_p145_l8">,
							by an executive decree; per <foreign xml:lang="lat">populum</foreign>,
							by a law. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uno_p145_l11">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">dolore</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="itaque_p145_l12">,
							etc.: an artful way of making the punishment of death seem less cruel;
							since death is a relief, these myths, says Cicero, have been invented to
							give it terror. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_remotis_p145_l15">:
							equiv. to a fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a); G. 593,2 ; H. 638, 2 (549, 2);
							H.-B. 578, 6. <milestone n="5" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mea_p145_l17">: § <ref n="AG 355">355</ref>,a (222,a); B. 211, i,a; G.381; H. 449, I (408,
							t 2); H.-B. 345. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="popularis_p145_l19">,
							not <gloss>popular</gloss> but <gloss>devoted to the people,
								democratic:</gloss> Caesar was now the recognized leader of this
							party. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctore_p145_l20">
							(abl. abs.), <gloss>proposer</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognitore_p145_l20">
							, <gloss>sponsor</gloss> (a legal term). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="majorum_p145_l24">:
							none of Caesar 5 ancestors were men of any distinction, although some
							distant relatives were prominent in public affairs in the time of Sulla;
							see note on <ref n="pl137.23">p.
								137, l. 23</ref>. He belonged, however, to one of the oldest
							patrician families. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obsidom_p145_l25">,
							i.e. he is pledged at all events to defend the state as against the
							conspirators. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interesset_p145_l27">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, d (287, d); H.-B. 482, I. <foreign xml:lang="lat">levitatem</foreign>, <gloss>want
								of principle</gloss>, i.e. of the steady purpose, or stability of
							character, implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								gravitas</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="saluti_p145_l28">,
							i.e. not <foreign xml:lang="lat">voluntati</foreign>: their interests,
							not their capricious wishes. <pb n="408"/>> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_neminem_p145_l29">:
							it is said that the person referred to was Q. Metellus Nepos, brother of
							Celer (see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.19">19</ref>), a partisan of Pompey and an enemy
							of Cicero. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dedit_p145_l31"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decrevit_p145_l31">
							, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adfecit_p145_l31">:
							i.e. gave his vote for these acts. With this, of course, his present
							action is inconsistent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_p146_l1"> has
							for antecedent the subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicarit_p146_l1">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_p146_l3"> , <gloss>the
							matter</gloss> (in general); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causa_p146_l3"> , <gloss>the
							issue</gloss> to be decided. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Caesar_p146_l3">:
							the full name gives emphasis, contrasting him with the <foreign xml:lang="lat">non nemo</foreign> (<ref n="pl145.29">p.
								145, l. 29</ref>). Caesar votes for a judgment against the
							conspirators which seems contrary to the Sempronian Law, but <emph>
								he</emph>, a true friend of the people (<foreign xml:lang="lat">vere popularis</foreign>), recognizes that this law
							applies to Roman citizens only, and that it therefore cannot protect
							these traitors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Semproniam_p146_l4">:
							see note on "Crucifixion," etc., <ref n="pl61.10">p. 61, l.
								10</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latorem_p146_l6">,
							i.e. C. Gracchus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jussu_populi_p146_l7">:
							not strictly true, for C. Gracchus was put to death, not <gloss>by
								order
								of the people</gloss>, but by virtue of the dictatorial authority
							conferred upon the consuls by the Senate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_publicae_p146_l7">:
							dative. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dependisse_p146_l7">:
							punishment with the Romans was regarded as a penalty <gloss>
								paid</gloss> by the offender to the injured party (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">dare,
								solvere, pendere</foreign> of the guilty; <foreign xml:lang="lat">capere,
								petere, repetere, postulare</foreign>, etc., of the person wronged). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lentulum_p146_l8">:
							by discussing this conspirator as an example of the would-be <foreign xml:lang="lat">popularis</foreign>, Cicero skillfully throws
							discredit on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">non nemo</foreign> (p. 145,
							l.29) and others like him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="largitorem_p146_l8">,
							etc., i.e. <gloss>however lavish</gloss>, — a symptom of courting the
							popular favor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_jactare_p146_l13">,
							i.e. as a pretended friend of liberty, like the <foreign xml:lang="lat">non
								nemo</foreign> above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnis_cruciattis_p146_l15">:
							accusative plural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamob_rem_p146_l17">,
							etc.: because Caesar's view has in Caesar a popular sponsor, while the
							view of Silanus is in fact the more merciful one. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="statueritis_p146_l17">
							, <foreign xml:lang="lat">dederitis</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c and N. (307, c and a.); G. 595, N.2; H.
							540, 2 (473, 2); cf. H.-B. 490. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contionem_p146_l18">:
							see Introd. to Manilian Law in notes (p.272). The action of the consul
							would have to be justified before the people, who might regard it as a
							tyrannical measure. In this justification Cicero would have Caesar to
							assist him. <pb n="409"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obtinebo_eam_p146_l20">
							, <gloss>make it appear that it</gloss> [this view], etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_liceat_p146_l23">:
							an asseveration like our "so help me God." The point lies in the idea of
							"so and not otherwise" implied in it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_moveor_p146_l24">
							, <gloss>as</gloss> [it is true that] <gloss>I am influenced</gloss>,
							etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="animo_p146_l29">,
							hr <gloss>my mind's eye</gloss> (properly, abl. of means). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patria_p146_l29"> , <gloss>native
							city</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_vero_p146_l32">
							: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_p146_l32">
							introduces (as often) the most striking point. The other conspirators
							are bad enough, <gloss>but when</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Vestalium_p147_l4">:
							see note on <ref n="pl130.19">p. 130, l. 19</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_quis_p147_l7">:
							for form, see § <ref n="AG 148">148</ref>, b, N. (104, a, N.); G. 106,
							R.; H. 512, I (454, I); cf. H-B. 141, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sumpserit_p147_l9">:
							see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">dependisse</foreign>, <ref n="pl146.7">p. 146, l. 7</ref>; for tense, see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">statueritis</foreign>, <ref n="pl146.17">p.
								146, l.
								17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_conlocarent_p147_l16">:
							purp. clause in appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">id</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_vero_p147_l22">,
							etc.: <foreign xml:lang="lat">reductio ad absurdum</foreign>, as usual
							with this phrase; § <ref n="AG 525">525</ref>, b, N. (315, b, N.); G.
							591, R.4; H.-B. 578, 31 a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Caesar_p147_l22">:
							L. Caesar (consul <date when="-0064">B.C. 64</date>), was a distant
							relative of the Dictator, son of Lucius Caesar (consul <date when="-0090">B.C. 90</date>, the year of the Social War), the author
							of the law giving citizenship to the Italian allies (see note, Arch.,
							sect. <ref n="Cic. Arch. 7">7</ref>). The sister of Lucius Caesar (the
							younger) was married to Lentulus, and his mother, Fulvia, was daughter
							of M. Fulvius Flaccus, the leading adherent of C. Gracchus. When
							Gracchus and Flaccus found themselves (<date when="-0121">B.C. 121</date>)
							drawn into a collision with the Senate, they sent the young son of
							Flaccus with a proposition of compromise. The Senate, however, refused
							to listen to any terms, threw the messenger into prison, — where he was
							afterwards strangled, —and moved upon the insurgents with all the power
							of the state. In the contest that followed, both leaders and several
							thousands of their partisans lost their lives. It was to these events
							that L. Caesar had appealed in justifying his vote in condemnation of
							his brother-in-law Lentulus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_p147_l26">
							refers to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="avum_p147_l26"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legatum_p147_l26">:
							of course the informal messenger of insurgents could have no claim to
							the title <gloss>ambassador</gloss>, or to the privileges which attached
							to the title in ancient as well as modern times. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_p147_l27">
							limits </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factum_p147_l27">:
							understand with simile some word describing the present conspiracy (<gloss>what
							act of theirs was like this?</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="largitionis_est_p147_l29">:
							the plans of C. Gracchus embraced not only a <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex
								frutmentaria</foreign>, allowing every citizen to buy a certain
							amount of corn from the state at less than half its market rate, and a <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex agraria</foreign>, providing for the distribution
							of public land among the poorer citizens, but also the establishment of
							several colonies, both in Italy and the provinces, the object of which
							was at once to provide poor citizens with land, and to relieve the city,
							by emigration, of a part of its proletariat. Though these grants were
							perhaps just, yet their proposal was regarded by the nobility as a
							political bid for popular favor, and hence gave rise to violent party
							jealousy (partium <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								contentio</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="avus_p147_l31">
							(see note on <ref n="pl131.6">p. 131, l. 6</ref>): he was an active
							supporter of the Senate on this occasion; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_p147_l31">
							(1.32) refers to the same person. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbem_inflammandam_p148_l4">:
							according to Sallust's Catiline, ch. 43, this work was assigned to
							Gabinius and Statilius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vereamini_p148_l5">
							follows </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="censeo_p148_l5">
							(ironical), as if with <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign> omitted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voces_p148_l12"> , <gloss>
							remarks</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_p148_l13"> , <gloss>on
							the part of those</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vereri_ut_p148_l13">:
							§ <ref n="AG 564">564</ref>(331, f); B. 296, 2; G. 550, 2; 11.567, I
							(498, iii, N.1); H.-B. 502, 4. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mea_p148_l16">,
							etc.: observe the antithesis between <foreign xml:lang="lat">mea
								summa
								cura</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">majore
								populi. . . voluntate</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_p148_l29"> , <gloss>
							for</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consentiunt_p148_l29">
							, <gloss>unite</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_ut_p149_l1">
							, <gloss>only to</gloss>, etc. (lit. <gloss>with this
								limitation that</gloss>): see § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, b (319,
							b); G. 552, R3; H-B. 521, 2, d. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summam_consilique_p149_l1">
							, <gloss>superiority in rank andprecedence in counsel</gloss> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_ordinis_p149_l3">
							(i.e. the Senate) limits <foreign xml:lang="lat">dissensione</foreign>
							in the sense of <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum hoc</foreign>, etc. For the
							long contest here alluded to, see Introduction, p. lxv. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_etc_p149_l5">
							, <gloss>and if we keep this union</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="confirmo_p149_l6">
							, <gloss>I assure</gloss>, in a different sense from <foreign xml:lang="lat">confirmatam</foreign>: Latin style does not (as ours
							does) object to such repetitions with a variation in meaning. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunos_aerarios_p149_l9">
							, <gloss>deans</gloss> if <gloss>the tribes</gloss>. The Roman people
							were divided into thirty-five tribes, local and territorial, like wards.
							These tribes were made the basis of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia
							centuriata</foreign>, as well as of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia
								tributa</foreign>. They served also as general administrative and
							financial divisions. From the latter character the name <foreign xml:lang="lat">tribuni aerarii</foreign> was given to their
							presiding officers. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scribas_p149_l10">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">scribae quaestorii</foreign> (treasury
							clerks) formed an important and powerful corporation. As they were a
							permanent body, while the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestors_p149_l10">
							(treasurers) were elected annually, they had the real responsibility in
							the management of the treasury. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sortis_p149_l11">:
							the quaestors entered upon office on the Nones of December (Dec. 5); all
							other patrician magistrates on the first of January. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">scribae</foreign> had therefore come together in
							order to be present while the quaestors drew lots for their provinces. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ingenuorum_p149_l12">,
							free-born. Freedmen, <foreign xml:lang="lat">libertini</foreign>, were
							always regarded as inferior in rank, if not in civil and political
							rights. Even these, however, are shown in the next chapter to be
							interested in the safety of the republic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sua_vktute_p149_l12">:
							manumission was very commonly bestowed as the reward of some peculiar
							merit in the slave. <pb n="412"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_nati_p149_l12">,
							i.e. citizens, as contrasted with the manumitted slaves (who were for
							the most part of foreign birth). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_sit_p149_l12">:
							§ <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, d (320, d); H.-B. 579, N.2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condicione_p149_l12">:
							§ <ref n="AG 415">415</ref>, N. (251, N.); Cf. B. 224, I; G. 400; H.
							473, 2, N.1 (419, 2 4); H.-B. 443. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluntatis_p149_l12">:
							partitive gen. with quantum, as if <foreign xml:lang="lat">tantum
							voluntatis quantum</foreign>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circum_tabernas_p149_l30">,
							i.e. among the artisans. The Roman shops were like little stalls along
							the street, open in front, with a "long room," or perhaps two, at the
							back. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cubile_lectulum_p150_l3">:
							both words mean nearly the same thing, and imply a very humble way of
							living. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="otiosum_p150_l4">
							, <gloss>
								peaceable</gloss>; so <foreign xml:lang="lat">oti</foreign> (1.6). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_p150_l8">
							relates to <foreign xml:lang="lat">eorum</foreign>, three lines above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incensis_p150_l9">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">tabernis</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="futurum_fuisset_p150_l9">;
							§ <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, d (308, d); G. 597, R3 H. 582 (51 I, 2);
							H-B. 581, a; the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied
							in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incensis_p150_l9_2">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populi_Romani_p150_l11">,
							as contrasted with the Senate: cf. the formula <foreign xml:lang="lat">Senatus
								Populusque Romanus</foreign>. <milestone n="151.2" unit="pg_l"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="impiae_p150_l11"> , <gloss>
							impious</gloss> (in its strict sense a want of filial duty). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arcem_Capitolium_p151_l4">:
							the Capitoline was a saddle-shaped hill, having the temple of Jupiter
							Capitolinus (Capitolium) on the southwestern point and the old citadel (<foreign xml:lang="lat">arx</foreign>) on the northeastern (see Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.20">20</ref>). Since Jupiter Capitolinus was the
							protecting divinity of Rome, his temple was the most sacred spot in the
							whole empire. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aras_Penatium_p151_l5">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Penates</foreign> were gods of the household
							and the larder (<foreign xml:lang="lat">penus</foreign>), worshipped by
							every <foreign xml:lang="lat">paterfamilias</foreign> in his own <foreign xml:lang="lat">atrium</foreign>. The state, being developed from the
							family, had likewise its Penates, which were fabled to have been brought
							by Aeneas from Troy and established at Lavinium, whence they were
							transferred to Alba Longa, and afterwards to Rome. Their temple was on
							the Velia, the low hill Connecting the Palatine and Esquiline. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignem_Vestue_p151_l5">:
							the temple of Vesta was on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sacra
								Via</foreign>, toward the Palatine, — a small round building
							containing the symbolic household fire of the Roman state. see note on
							the Vestals, <ref n="pl130.19">p. 130, l. 19</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="focis_p151_l9">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">focus</foreign>, the symbol of household
							life, was a brazier for burning charcoal. It originally stood at the
							rear end of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">atrium</foreign>, or great hall, of the house. Later
							it was moved, for all practical purposes, to the kitchen, but a
							representative focus remained in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">atrium</foreign> and continued to be the symbol of
							household life. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_facultas_p151_l11">:
							§ <ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, e (201, d); cf. B. 251, 4, b; G. 616, 2;
							H.-B. 327. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_causa_p151_l13">
							, <gloss>on a political question</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quantis_delerit_p151_l14">:
							this clause will be best turned into English by translating the
							participles </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fundatum_p151_l14">,
							etc., as verbs, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delerit_p151_l14">
							as a relative clause, — <gloss>with how great toil this empire was
							established, <emph>which</emph> one night</gloss>, etc. In Latin the
							question is contained in the interrogative modifiers of imperium and not
							in the main clause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_factorum_p151_l29">:
							for cases, see § <ref n="AG 354">354</ref>, b (22 I, b); B. 209, I; G.
							377; 11.457(409, iii); H-B. 352, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gesta_p152_l1">:
							abl. abs. with re <foreign xml:lang="lat">publica</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Scipio_p152_l3">:
							the elder Africanus, who brought the Second Punic War to a triumphant
							close by the battle of Zama, <date when="-0202">B.C.
								202</date>. By "carrying the war into Africa," he forced Hannibal to
							retire from Italy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alter_Africanus_p152_l5">:
							the younger, surnamed Aemilianus. He was the son of L. Aemilius Paulus
							(mentioned below), and adopted by the son of the elder Africanus. He
							captured Carthage, <date when="-0146">B.C. 146</date>, and Numantia, in
							Spain, <date when="-0133">B.C. 133</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Paulus_p152_l7">:
							father of the younger Africanus, and, like his son, the most eminent and
							upright man of his generation. He brought the Third Macedonian War to a
							close by the battle of Pydna, <date when="-0168">B.C. 168</date>, and led King Perseus captive in his
							triumphal procession. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="currum_p152_l7"> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">triumphalem</foreign>]: the captives did not go with
							or behind the triumphal chariot, but preceded it in the procession. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bis_liberavit_p152_l9">:
							by the victories over the German invaders, —over the Teutones at Aquae
							Sextiae (<date when="-0102">B.C. 102</date>), and the Cimbri at
							Vercellae (<date when="-0101">B.C. 101</date>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Pompeius_p152_l10">:
							it should be remembered that Pompey was now in the East, in the midst of
							his career of conquest, and that his return was looked for with
							expectancy by all parties. Cicero took every means to win the confidence
							of the great general, and gain him over to his views in public affairs;
							but to no purpose. After some wavering, Pompey associated himself with
							Caesar, thus giving the Senate a blow from which it never recovered, and
							preparing the way for his own downfall. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquid_loci_p152_l13">:
							§ <ref n="AG 346">346</ref>, a, 3 (216, a, 3); B. 202, 2; G. 369; H.
							442 (397, 3); H.-B. 346. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_p152_l16">
							, <gloss>and yet</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uno_loco_p152_l16">
							, <gloss>in one respect</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oppressi_serviunt_p152_l18">
							, <gloss>are crushed and enslaved</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 496">
								496</ref>, N.2 (292, R.); cf. B. 337,2; H-B. 604, I. <milestone n="11" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_impeno_p153_l1">
							, <gloss>in place if</gloss>: all these honors, which Cicero might have
							gained by a foreign command, he has renounced in order to stay at home
							and protect the city. <pb n="415"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="clientelis_hospitusque_p153_l4">:
							the relation of <foreign xml:lang="lat">cliens</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="lat">patronus</foreign> was that of a subordinate to a
							superior, carrying with it services on the one side and protection on
							the other; the <foreign xml:lang="lat">hospites</foreign> were, on the
							other hand, equals, and their connection was one of mutual aid and
							friendship. Foreign states and citizens were eager to form such ties
							with influential Romans, and they were equally advantageous to the
							Roman. Of course a provincial governor had peculiar opportunities for
							this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbanis_opibus_p153_l5">
							, <gloss>the means afforded by a city life</gloss>. Such ties would be
							more easily formed by a sojourn in a province, but they could also be
							formed by a statesman who remained at home; for the value of such a
							relation to the provincial consisted in the opportunities for protection
							and assistance which the statesman possessed in the city itself. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_studiis_p153_l6">
							, <gloss>in return for my efforts</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_dum_p153_l10">
							, <gloss>and as long as this</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mentibus_p153_l10">:
							§ <ref n="AG 429">429</ref>, 3 (254, a); cf. B. 228, I; H. 485, I
							(425, 12); H.-B. 436. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suo_solius_p153_l15">:
							§ <ref n="AG 302">302</ref>, e (197, e); B. 243, 31 a; G. 321, R.2; H.
							446, 3 (398, 3); H.-B. 339, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_qui_p153_l20">
							, <gloss>a consul who</gloss>, etc.: § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, d
							(102, d); B. 247, I, a; H.-B. 271, ii. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_praestare_p153_l22">
							, <gloss>make good</gloss> (so far as he may) <gloss>on his own
								part</gloss>
						</gloss>
